title: Anatomy of a self-made star
gist: On the centenary of Marilyn Monroe's birth, Prospect's DAT Green reviews two London shows — the BFI's 'Self-Made Star' film season and the National Portrait Gallery's photography retrospective — arguing they reveal Monroe as a self-taught 'genius' who deliberately resisted cashing in on her comedic-bombshell persona, fought Twentieth Century Fox for creative control, and remains under-recognized for her dramatic and post-1956 work.

themes.theme: Constructing and resisting the comedic bombshell persona

insights.claim: Monroe's four best-known comedies — Gentlemen Prefer Blondes (1953), How to Marry a Millionaire (1953), The Seven Year Itch (1955) and Some Like It Hot (1959) — fixed the 'comedic blonde bombshell' cultural icon most people know her as, a deliberate construct comparable to Chaplin's Tramp, Keaton's or Sellers's personas.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Frames Monroe's public image as an engineered brand rather than an accident of casting — a template for thinking about star-persona construction as a durable, monetizable asset.
insights.locator: You could play it safe and choose the four great comedies

insights.claim: Director Billy Wilder wanted Monroe to simply keep exploiting the comic persona she had already built, but Monroe chose not to monetize the 'gold-digger' stereotype further even though doing so was in her direct economic and commercial interest.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: An early example of an artist deliberately forgoing short-term commercial upside to protect long-term creative range — a pattern worth noting whenever a bankable persona is being pushed by studio/label incentives.
insights.locator: When her comedy persona became such a hit

insights.claim: Biographer Andrew Wilson, speaking at the BFI on Monroe's actual centenary date, compared her constructed on-screen character directly to Chaplin's Tramp — a deliberate, sustained artistic construction rather than an authentic self.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Reinforces the thesis that Monroe's stardom was engineered craft, not luck — relevant context for valuing her legacy IP and the current centenary exhibitions.

insights.claim: The BFI season highlights Monroe's first starring role in the 1948 B-movie Ladies of the Chorus, including a scene the reviewer calls one of the most disconcerting in any Monroe film: a row of dancing girls crouched manipulating toy dolls to dance alongside them.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Shows the BFI retrospective is deliberately surfacing overlooked early material rather than just replaying the greatest hits — useful signal for what the centenary programming values.
insights.quote: the row of dancing girls crouched with toy dolls being manipulated to dance too
insights.locator: The BFI shows her in her first starring role

insights.claim: Before her comedic breakthrough, Monroe built her career on straight dramatic roles: a small part opposite Bette Davis in All About Eve (1950), the reviewer's pick for her best film, Don't Bother to Knock (1952) playing a psychopathic babysitter, and her star-making turn in Niagara (1953).
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Corrects the popular record of Monroe as purely a comedic star — her dramatic range preceded and enabled the comedic fame, a distinction relevant to how her legacy/IP should be marketed and curated.
insights.locator: The BFI also make a point of showing Monroe in straight dramatic roles

themes.theme: Fighting Fox and the underused post-1956 freedom

insights.claim: Monroe took on and defeated Twentieth Century Fox in a dispute that was not only about money but, critically, about creative control — winning the right to approve the directors and cinematographers she worked with.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: An early, successful instance of a contracted studio talent winning creative-control rights from a major studio — a precedent-setting labor/IP dispute worth understanding in the context of talent-vs-studio power struggles.
insights.locator: Elsewhere in Prospect, I have set out

insights.claim: Despite winning full creative and commercial freedom after 1956, Monroe made only a handful of films before her likely-accidental death in 1962, of which only the Wilder-directed Some Like It Hot left a strong mark on popular memory.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Frames her post-1956 output as an underused asset — the current BFI/NPG retrospective is explicitly working to rehabilitate this under-known late period.
insights.locator: One open question is why, when Monroe secured this commercial and creative freedom

insights.claim: The Misfits (1961), Monroe's last completed film, was also Clark Gable's last film and depicts the decline of the American cowboy, including distressing scenes of cruelty to horses that are resolved with dramatic, humane satisfaction between Gable's and Monroe's characters.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Positions The Misfits, now back in UK/Irish cinemas, as a historically significant but under-known final work worth reassessing as part of Monroe's serious dramatic legacy.
insights.quote: It is a film of lasts
insights.locator: And seeing The Misfits in a cinema is an utter revelation

insights.claim: The Prince and the Showgirl (1957) was co-produced by Monroe's own company, Marilyn Monroe Productions, and on the big screen she more than holds her own against her co-star Laurence Olivier.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Evidence that her post-Fox creative-control win translated into real producing power and screen command, not just contractual freedom on paper.
insights.quote: more than holds her own with her thespian costar, Laurence Olivier.
insights.locator: Thorndyke’s observation, quoted above, is true

themes.theme: Camera genius and the self-made-star thesis

insights.claim: Sybil Thorndyke, Monroe's co-star in The Prince and the Showgirl, told fellow cast members that the company 'needed her desperately' because she was the only one among them who knew how to act in front of a camera.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: A first-hand testimonial from a veteran co-star that Monroe possessed a distinct, camera-specific technical skill rather than generic star charisma.
insights.quote: We need her desperately. She is the only one of us who knows how to act in front of a camera.
insights.locator: Sybil Thorndyke, who starred with her

insights.claim: Jack Cardiff, cinematographer on The Prince and the Showgirl, said Monroe wasn't merely a great actress but a genius whose talent was often invisible off-camera and only revealed once filmed.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: A technical practitioner's assessment (not a fan's) that Monroe's gift was specifically optical/photogenic, reinforcing the case for valuing the NPG photography retrospective as much as the film season.
insights.quote: There’s no doubt she had a genius. She wasn’t a great actress, she was a genius.
insights.locator: Jack Cardiff, the cinematographer on that same film

insights.claim: The National Portrait Gallery exhibition groups Monroe's portraits by photographer, with the hanging of André de Dienes's Blanket series singled out by the reviewer as breathtaking.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: Signals the NPG show's curatorial approach treats Monroe as an active creative collaborator with named photographers, not a passive subject — relevant to how her image rights/legacy are being curated and valued.
insights.quote: the presentation of the Blanket series by André de Dienes, for example, is breathtaking
insights.locator: Some of the hanging is astonishing

insights.claim: Monroe came to stardom from outside the movie and celebrity system as a self-taught 'autodidact' — she paid for her own acting lessons with money she could barely afford and listened to Ella Fitzgerald to teach herself to sing.
insights.speaker: DAT Green
insights.so_what: The article's central verdict: Monroe's career is best understood as self-built craft accumulation rather than studio manufacture — the frame both exhibitions are built around for her centenary.
insights.quote: She was an autodidact who made it.
insights.locator: She was a model with ambition
